title: Anglo-Indian fish curry
subtitle: White fish in a spiced tomato and coconut milk sauce
excerpt: Firm white fish fillets poached in a sauce of onion, tomato, and coconut milk spiced with mustard seeds, turmeric, and fresh chilli. The Anglo-Indian version of the coastal fish curries of Goa and Madras — restrained, aromatic, and cooked without marinating.
sourceNotes: Based on Anglo-Indian fish curry traditions documented in Lizzie Collingham, 'Curry: A Tale of Cooks and Conquerors' (2005) and Cecilia Leong-Salobir, 'Food Culture in Colonial Asia' (2011). The Anglo-Indian fish curry draws on coastal south Indian technique but is modified for British kitchens: coconut milk replaces fresh coconut, and the sauce is built on canned tomatoes rather than tamarind.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Sauce can be made 2 days ahead and refrigerated. Add the raw fish and poach when ready to serve.

content.text: The Anglo-Indian fish curry reached Britain through the domestic cookbooks and club menus of the colonial period, carrying the flavours of coastal south India (mustard seeds, curry leaves, coconut milk, turmeric) filtered through kitchens that substituted tinned goods for fresh ones and adjusted the heat levels for an Anglophone audience. What remained is a mild, fragrant sauce in which firm white fish poaches gently for the last ten minutes of cooking.

Cod, hake, or pollock all work well — firm enough to hold together in the sauce, mild enough not to compete with the spicing. The fish goes in raw at the end; overcooking is the only real mistake this recipe offers.

items.prepNote: skin off, cut into large chunks; hake or pollock also work
or 1 fresh green chilli, finely sliced

content.text: Heat {{sunflower-oil}} of oil in a wide saucepan over a medium-high heat. Add {{mustard-seeds}} of mustard seeds and {{curry-leaves}} curry leaves. When the seeds begin to pop, add {{onion}} sliced onion and cook for 8 minutes until golden. Add {{garlic}} crushed garlic, {{ginger-fresh}} grated ginger, {{turmeric-ground}} of turmeric, and {{chilli-flakes}} of chilli. Cook for 2 minutes, stirring. Add {{tinned-tomatoes}} of tinned tomatoes and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Simmer for 10 minutes until the sauce has reduced slightly.
Add the fish and coconut milk

Stir in {{coconut-milk}} of coconut milk and bring to a gentle simmer. Lay {{cod-fillet}} of fish chunks into the sauce in a single layer. Cover and cook on a low heat for 8–10 minutes until the fish is just opaque throughout and flakes when pressed. Squeeze {{lemon}} of lemon juice over and scatter with {{coriander-fresh}} fresh coriander. Serve with basmati rice.

items.symptom: Fish breaks apart in the sauce
items.cause: Overcooked, or fish stirred rather than left in place
items.fix: Once the fish goes in, resist the urge to stir. Cook covered on a low heat and check progress by pressing gently on top of a piece with a spoon.

items.cause: Tomatoes not reduced enough before adding the coconut milk
items.fix: Simmer uncovered for 5 more minutes to reduce. The sauce should coat the back of a spoon.

items.cause: Oil not hot enough when seeds are added
items.fix: The oil should be shimmering before the seeds go in. Use a lid to contain the seeds if they pop vigorously.

content.text: Fish curry occupied a specific place in Anglo-Indian cooking as a Friday dish and a lighter option alongside the heavier meat curries. The Goan and Madrasi coastal traditions of cooking fish in coconut-based sauces were among the most accessible for British cooks to adopt: the techniques were similar to European poaching and the results were reliably clean and fragrant. By the mid-twentieth century, versions of this dish had entered mainstream British cooking through Anglo-Indian households and the first South Asian restaurants.
